figures.caption: Figure 1 Conceptual comparison of self-evolution paradigms for LLM agents. SEAL closes the loop between policy learning and environment adaptation by using verifier-grounded failure diagnosis as the shared feedback signal.

Table 3 Ablation of SEAL on Qwen2.5-7B. Leave-one-out ablations show that environment-side adaptation, diagnosisguided reweighting, and closed-loop updates all contribute, with Full SEAL outperforming all single-sided variants.

Figure 3 Training dynamics comparing Vanilla RL and SEAL on BFCL V3 validation subsets. We plot validation accuracy across training steps for Base, Missing Functions, Missing Parameters, and Long Context. Shaded regions denote binomial standard error estimated from each 100-sample validation subset.
Table 4 Main hyperparameter configuration for BFCL experiments. Unless otherwise specified, Vanilla RL and SEAL use the same optimization, rollout, and evaluation settings.

Table 1 Main results on the BFCL V3 multi-turn benchmark. Under the same 400-sample training budget, SEAL consistently improves 3B–8B backbones over Vanilla RL. Reference models are included only to contextualize the broader performance range of existing tool-use systems.

Figure 2 SEAL training-time co-evolution loop. The agent interacts with an executable tool environment to produce multi-turn trajectories, which are diagnosed into verifier-grounded failure types. These diagnoses jointly drive learninginterface evolution through adapted training observations and policy optimization through diagnosis-guided trajectory weighting, while keeping tool semantics and evaluation fixed.
Table 5 Priority order for assigning diagnostic labels when multiple failure signals are present.
Table 6 Diagnostic utility weights used by SEAL.

Table 2 Out-of-distribution generalization on BFCL V4 and 𝜏2-bench. Models are trained only on the 400-sample BFCL V3 training set and evaluated on held-out task distributions to measure cross-benchmark transfer.
